does: Safe-SAIL develops a framework for systematically interpreting sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to large language models with focus on safety domains. The paper introduces a pre-explanation evaluation metric to identify which SAEs generate the finest-grained, most interpretable safety-related features, then reduces the computational cost of explaining these features by 55% through a segment-level simulation strategy. The authors train and evaluate a comprehensive suite of SAEs across four safety domains—pornography, politics, violence, and terror—producing 1,758 human-readable feature explanations with systematic safety evaluations, creating the first large-scale mechanistic safety interpretability resource for LLMs.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in sparse autoencoder interpretability that decompose dense neural network activations into interpretable monosemantic features. It extends prior SAE research by addressing the specific challenge of safety domains, which are low-frequency concept areas where standard SAE training may fail to produce coherent features. The paper responds to limitations in prior mechanistic interpretability work that focused on common, high-frequency concepts. Safe-SAIL also draws on existing safety evaluation frameworks while combining them with feature-level interpretation, bridging the gap between interpretability-focused and safety-focused branches of LLM research.
enables: Safe-SAIL establishes infrastructure for fine-grained mechanistic understanding of how LLMs process and generate harmful content, enabling better debugging and control strategies for safety. The 1,758 explained safety features and evaluation methodology allow future work to identify which model components drive harmful behaviors and test interventions at the feature level. This foundation supports developing more targeted safety techniques such as feature-level steering, activation patching for safety, and automated detection of latent safety failure modes. The framework also enables researchers to study how safety concepts emerge during training and to benchmark new SAE architectures specifically on their safety interpretability capabilities.
